title: Cross-Linguistic Syntactic Difference in Multilingual BERT: How Good is It and How Does It Affect Transfer?

round-A_JuniorNano_output.reasoning: This work targets syntactic knowledge in a Transformer-based language model (mBERT). It empirically analyzes syntax by using Universal Dependencies to define grammatical relations, computing head-dependent differences d(head,dep) from mBERT representations, and testing a linear classifier to recover the relation from these differences. It introduces a cross-linguistic syntactic difference measure by comparing joint distributions (feature x = d(head,dep), label y = grammatical relation) with the Optimal Transport Dataset Distance, and validates this measure by showing high correlation with a formal syntactic distance based on World Atlas of Language Structures (Jaccard distance on parameter sets) (rho about 0.80 in layer 7). It evaluates zero-shot transfer for dependency parsing via LAS, analyzes pretraining and fine-tuning effects on the syntactic difference, and reports that pretraining-derived differences strongly correlate with transfer difficulty, while fine-tuning generally reduces the distance and improves transfer. It also uses a gradient-boosting regression on typological morphosyntactic features to predict the pretraining transfer gap, achieving R2 around 0.85 and a rho around 0.89 between predicted and computed syntactic differences, with word-order features among the most influential. Overall, the paper provides a clear empirical analysis of syntactic knowledge in a Transformer model and its cross-lingual transfer, aligning representation-space differences with linguistic typology. One-sentence justification: The study explicitly analyzes syntactic knowledge in a Transformer LM with empirical syntax-focused experiments and cross-lingual transfer evaluation, satisfying the inclusion criteria.
